title: Adenomyosis: endometrial glands within the myometrium
vignette: A 42-year-old woman, gravida 4 para 4, comes to the office due to heavy and painful menstrual bleeding over the past 3 months. The patient's last menstrual period was 3 weeks ago. Menarche was at age 10, and menstrual periods last for 3-5 days and occur every 30 days. She is sexually active with her husband and does not have pain with intercourse. The patient had a bilateral tubal ligation 3 years ago after the birth of her last child. She takes no medications and has no allergies. BMI is 24 kg/m2. Vital signs are normal. On bimanual examination, the uterus is uniformly enlarged and tender. Urine beta-hCG is negative. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

clues.note: Older multiparous reproductive-age women are the classic demographic for adenomyosis. Repeated pregnancy and the higher cumulative estrogen exposure of the late reproductive years favor invasion of endometrial tissue into the myometrium.

clues.text: heavy and painful menstrual bleeding over the past 3 months
clues.note: Menorrhagia + dysmenorrhea (heavy AND painful periods) is the core symptom pair of adenomyosis. The ectopic endometrium inside the muscle bleeds cyclically and disrupts myometrial contraction, causing both the heavy flow and the cramping.

clues.text: does not have pain with intercourse
clues.note: No dyspareunia helps steer AWAY from endometriosis, where deep dyspareunia and cul-de-sac/ovarian implants are typical. Adenomyosis pain is diffuse menstrual cramping, not deep penetration pain.

clues.text: uterus is uniformly enlarged and tender
clues.note: A symmetrically (uniformly/globally) enlarged, boggy, tender uterus is the exam hallmark of adenomyosis. Contrast with leiomyoma, which gives an ASYMMETRIC, irregular, lumpy-bumpy but typically non-tender uterus.
A negative pregnancy test removes pregnancy-related causes of bleeding and a uterine mass (e.g., gestation, ectopic), focusing the differential on structural benign uterine disease.

whatMatters: Strip the story to: 40s, multiparous, heavy + painful periods, no dyspareunia, and a uniformly enlarged, tender uterus with a negative pregnancy test. That pattern is adenomyosis. The asked histology is ectopic endometrial GLANDS and STROMA within the myometrium - answer C.
strategy: For an enlarged uterus with abnormal bleeding, separate the four big benign entities by uterine shape and pain: adenomyosis (uniform, tender, heavy+painful), leiomyoma (asymmetric/irregular, often non-tender), endometrial polyp (focal intracavitary growth, intermenstrual bleeding), and endometrial hyperplasia (unopposed estrogen, increased gland:stroma ratio). Match the exam description to the histology offered.

options.text: Benign myometrial smooth muscle cell proliferation
Blastocyst implantation in the fallopian tube
Endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium
Localized overgrowth of endometrium into the uterine cavity
Unregulated endometrial proliferation with increased gland-to-stroma ratio

eliminations.label: leiomyoma (fibroid) - wrong uterine shape
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: benign monoclonal smooth muscle proliferation = uterine leiomyoma, the most common cause of an enlarged uterus with menorrhagia. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: fibroids make the uterus ASYMMETRIC, irregular, and typically non-tender; this uterus is uniformly enlarged AND tender, which fits adenomyosis. ✅ Correct if: the stem said firm, irregular, lumpy/asymmetric uterus or discrete masses on imaging.

eliminations.label: ectopic pregnancy - excluded by negative hCG
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: blastocyst implantation in the fallopian tube = ectopic pregnancy. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: urine beta-hCG is negative and she had a bilateral tubal ligation; there is no pregnancy and the picture is chronic cyclic bleeding, not acute tubal rupture. ✅ Correct if: positive hCG with unilateral pelvic pain and adnexal mass.

eliminations.label: endometrial polyp - wrong location of growth
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: focal overgrowth of endometrium projecting INTO the uterine cavity = endometrial polyp, a cause of intermenstrual/postmenopausal bleeding. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: polyps grow into the cavity, not into the muscle wall, and usually don't cause a uniformly enlarged tender uterus with dysmenorrhea. ✅ Correct if: intermenstrual spotting with a focal intracavitary lesion on sonohysterography.

eliminations.label: endometrial hyperplasia - wrong risk profile
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: unopposed-estrogen-driven proliferation with an increased gland-to-stroma ratio = endometrial hyperplasia, a precursor to endometrial carcinoma. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this requires chronic unopposed estrogen (obesity, PCOS, anovulation); she has regular ovulatory cycles, normal BMI, and a tender enlarged uterus pointing to adenomyosis. ✅ Correct if: obese/anovulatory patient with postmenopausal or irregular bleeding and a thickened endometrial stripe.

reasoning: 40s multiparous woman with heavy AND painful periods
Uniformly enlarged, tender uterus (not irregular like fibroids)
Negative hCG removes pregnancy -> ectopic endometrium within myometrium = adenomyosis -> answer C
mechanism: Adenomyosis is the presence of endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium. The heterotopic endometrium responds to cyclic estrogen/progesterone, bleeding into the muscle and provoking surrounding smooth-muscle hypertrophy and hyperplasia, which produces a diffusely enlarged, boggy, tender uterus with menorrhagia and dysmenorrhea. It differs from endometriosis (endometrial tissue OUTSIDE the uterus) and from leiomyoma (a discrete smooth-muscle tumor). Definitive diagnosis is historically on hysterectomy specimen; MRI/ultrasound can suggest it.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Gynecology > Uterine conditions: adenomyosis (endometrial glands/stroma in myometrium, uniformly enlarged tender uterus, menorrhagia+dysmenorrhea) vs leiomyoma vs endometriosis vs endometrial hyperplasia/polyp. Treatment: GnRH agonists, levonorgestrel IUD, definitive = hysterectomy.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The question asks for the underlying pathology, given a classic exam description. Map 'uniformly enlarged + tender + heavy/painful' to adenomyosis before reading the answer wording.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on adenomyosis vs endometriosis, gravida/para, menorrhagia, dysmenorrhea, gland-to-stroma ratio, myometrium? Tell me which and I'll define it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Leiomyoma (A) is the reflex 'enlarged uterus' answer and traps many. The tie-breaker here is UNIFORM + TENDER (adenomyosis) vs irregular/non-tender (fibroid).

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: You picked C and nailed it - bank the uterus-shape table: uniform+tender = adenomyosis; irregular = fibroid.

keywords.front: Uniformly enlarged, tender uterus + heavy painful periods

keywords.back: Endometrial glands and stroma WITHIN the myometrium

keywords.front: Endometrial tissue OUTSIDE the uterus + dyspareunia

keywords.back: Hysterectomy (GnRH agonist / LNG-IUD for symptom control)

firstAid.detail: Gynecology > Uterine pathology: definition, uniformly enlarged tender uterus, menorrhagia/dysmenorrhea, treatment.
firstAid.location: Gynecology > Uterine pathology: definition, uniformly enlarged tender uterus, menorrhagia/dysmenorrhea, treatment.

firstAid.detail: Gyn: leiomyoma, endometrial polyp, endometrial hyperplasia (gland:stroma ratio), endometriosis - compare features and histology.
firstAid.location: Gyn: leiomyoma, endometrial polyp, endometrial hyperplasia (gland:stroma ratio), endometriosis - compare features and histology.

firstAid.detail: Gyn: PALM-COEIN, role of beta-hCG, ultrasound/sonohysterography, endometrial biopsy indications.
firstAid.location: Gyn: PALM-COEIN, role of beta-hCG, ultrasound/sonohysterography, endometrial biopsy indications.

memoryHook: ADENOmyosis = endometrium 'ADDS IN' to the muscle wall -> uterus globally big, boggy, tender, and bleeds heavily.
